nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, flowing waters over sandy or muddy substrates in major river channels and tributaries

diet.notes: Sinking pellets, frozen bloodworms, blackworms, and Tubifex are generally well accepted. Will readily consume small tankmates that fit in its mouth. Has been known to overeat, so portion control is advisable.

avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed

summary: The Pictus Catfish (Pimelodus pictus) is a fast-moving silver catfish with bold black spots and long, trailing barbels, native to the Amazon and Orinoco river basins. It is best kept in groups of three or more and will prey on any tankmate small enough to swallow.
careNotes:
Pictus Catfish tend to be most active at dusk, dawn, and through the night (the low-light periods known as crepuscular hours), though they will venture out during the day in dimly lit tanks with adequate hiding spots. They are scaleless and thus sensitive to copper-based medications — always use copper-free treatments. Their very long barbels are easily damaged by sharp decor; smooth driftwood, rounded rocks, and soft substrate are strongly preferred.

This species is commonly sold with an inflated size claim of up to 25 cm, which appears to reflect misidentification with larger related pimelodids. The true adult maximum is around 11–13 cm.

Small tankmates such as neon tetras, ember tetras, guppies, and similar nano fish are at serious risk of being eaten, particularly at night. A minimum group of three individuals is advisable; single specimens tend to hide constantly and show stress. They are active, fast swimmers and appreciate a long tank footprint (120 cm, or roughly 4 ft, minimum) with open midwater swimming space alongside caves and driftwood.

Strong filtration and regular water changes (around 25–30% weekly) are important for long-term health. In well-maintained aquariums, captive lifespans of more than eight years have been reported; at least one individual is documented to have reached around 18 years.

Pictus Catfish have been used successfully as dither fish in cichlid tanks, helping reduce cichlid shyness, provided tankmates are too large to be swallowed. Their fin spines can entangle in standard fish nets — use a container or soft mesh net when moving them.
breedingNotes: Breeding in the home aquarium has not been reliably documented; commercial production is believed to rely on hormone-induced spawning. Setting up a home breeding attempt is considered impractical.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (12 cm), water parameters (22–25°C, pH 5.8–6.8, 1–15°H), tank dimensions, native range, and diet. Tank spec: 120×45×45 cm (243 L). Noted scaleless/copper-sensitive, not bred in captivity.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 11.0 cm, temperature 22–25°C, pH 6.0–8.0, family Pimelodidae, order Siluriformes. Lifespan typically 4–8 years in captivity; exceptional case documented at 18 years.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~12.7 cm), temperature (23.9–27.2°C), grouping recommendation (3–4 fish), lifespan (8+ years), and nocturnal behavior. Tank size recommendation: 55–150 gal depending on group size.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable home aquarium breeding documented. Commercial breeding is believed to use hormone injection.
